frameworks.name: predictive coding
frameworks.one_line: hierarchical inhibition of predictable input via prediction and error units

frameworks.name: energy-efficient recurrent neural networks
frameworks.one_line: RNN trained to minimise energy in predictive environments

core_question: Does predictive coding architecture need to be hard-wired, or can it emerge from an energy-efficiency objective?
key_assumptions: neural energy consumption is a meaningful training objective
sensory environment is predictable on multiple timescales
recurrent connectivity is sufficient to support prediction and error signalling
manipulations: training objective: minimise energy consumption in predictive environments
varied prediction timescale (fast lateral vs slower integrative)
key_findings: networks self-organise into prediction and error units with appropriate excitatory/inhibitory connectivity
trained networks inhibit predictable sensory input
predictions operate on two timescales: fast lateral and slower integrative
interpretations: predictive coding can emerge as a consequence of energy efficiency rather than architectural hard-wiring
top-down signals are not the sole source of prediction; lateral sensory prediction also contributes
